slides.title: Feed, focus & fresh starts
slides.body:
**Every job burns feed — that's tokens, and tokens are money**

- The dog only holds **so much in its head** (the context window)
- A long, rambling all-day chat → it loses the thread. **Start a fresh, rested dog.**
- A sloppy question to the Reservoir = trampling *every* field to find one sheep
- It's **weak at exact sums** and not perfectly repeatable — all the more reason to check
slides.notes: Two quick, money-saving habits. First: the dog can only hold so much in its head at once. In one long rambling all-day conversation it starts losing the thread and getting muddled — so when a chat goes sideways, don't push on, start a fresh rested dog with a new chat. Second: when we query our big data Reservoir, a vague 'give me everything' is the dog trampling every field to find one sheep, and that actually costs real money because it scans millions of rows. Ask tight, specific questions. And know the dog's honest weaknesses — it's bad at exact maths and you can ask the same thing twice and get slightly different answers. Which is the perfect setup for the most important habit of all.

slides.title: Begin with the end
slides.body:
**Know what a good harvest looks like before you open the gate**

- Picture the prize first: what does a *good* answer actually look like?
- A good fitment answer names the bike *and* why the part doesn't fit
- A good sales number = last week's net sales from the marts, query shown
- Write it in *one sentence* — now you've got something to check against

> Most bad AI output isn't wrong — it's *aimed at the wrong target.*
slides.notes: Here's a tip that'll change how you work, and it's Callum's favourite. Before you ever send the dog out, picture the prize — decide what a genuinely good result looks like. A good fitment answer names the bike and the actual reason the part doesn't fit. A good sales number is last week's net sales from the right data, with the query shown. Write that down in one sentence first. Because now, when the dog trots back, you've got something concrete to hold its work against. Most bad AI output isn't really wrong — it's just aimed at the wrong target. Define good up front.
slides.demo: In Claude, before asking your real question, type: "Before you answer, restate in one line what a complete, correct answer to this would include."

slides.title: TRACE — the gate-check
slides.body:
**You never shut the gate without counting the flock**

- **T — Traceable:** did it actually look, or just describe what it *would* do?
- **R — Real:** are the SKUs, orders, prices genuine and findable?
- **A — Accurate to intent:** did it answer the *exact* question?
- **C — Contained:** read-only? No customer PII? No Reservoir write?
- **E — Enough to act:** would you send it / publish it as-is?
slides.notes: And this is the through-line of the whole course — TRACE. You'd never shut the gate on your flock without counting them and checking they're yours and sound. TRACE is that same gate-check for anything the AI hands you. Traceable — did it actually go and look, or just describe what it would do. Real — are the numbers genuine and findable. Accurate to intent — did it answer the exact question you asked. Contained — was it read-only, no customer private data, no writing to our shared data. And Enough — would you actually act on this as it stands. Five seconds at the gate. Every single time.

slides.title: Now go practice
slides.body:
**Your turn — Session 1 missions are live in the app**

- Each of you has a mission for your real job
- One idea, one real task, one TRACE check
- The dog does the legwork — *you* count the flock
- Closed-book goal: "I could do this Monday with the app shut."

*Open the app → Session 1 → your mission.*
slides.notes: That's the foundation — now the only way this sticks is by doing it. Open the app, go to Session 1, and you'll each find a mission built around your actual job. They're short: one idea, one real task, and you finish by running TRACE on what comes back. Don't aim to see every feature — aim for 'I could genuinely do this on Monday with the app closed.' I'll be walking round, so grab me. Off you go.

2.title: Claude that acts — and the one rule
2.subtitle: Session 2: agentic Claude and the Reservoir. Going from talking about your data to working on it — safely.

slides.title: Session 2: Claude that acts — and the one rule
slides.body:
Welcome back to the Duck Pond.

- Last week: what this thing is
- This week: what it can DO
- And the one rule that keeps the whole farm safe
slides.notes: Welcome back, everyone. Last week we got our heads around what this thing actually is — a brilliant young sheepdog that doesn't know our farm yet. This week we open the gates and let it into our real fields, so it stops talking about our data and starts working on it. And there's one rule that comes with that — one rule that protects the whole business — and by the end of today every one of you will be able to say it in your own words.

slides.title: Where we're going today
slides.body:
Five stops in about 25 minutes:

- A quick recap, then: what can Claude actually SEE?
- The farm logbook (memory)
- THE RULE: the shared water trough
- Keeping the farm safe
- Then you go and practise
slides.notes: Here's the shape of the next twenty-five minutes. We'll recap last week in a sentence, then get hands-on with what Claude can actually see in our systems. We'll cover the farm logbook — how it remembers — then the big one, the one rule about our shared data. We'll finish with how we keep the farm safe, a real example, and then I hand it over to you to practise your own missions in the app. Nothing technical. Just farm sense.

slides.title: Recap: from whistling to opening the gates
slides.body:
Three ways to work the dog:

- Whistle from the yard = plain prompting
- Walk it round the field = giving it context
- Open the gates = connectors

Today is all about opening the gates.
slides.notes: Quick recap. Last week we said there are three ways to work the dog. Whistling from the yard is plain prompting — it's just got your whistle and its general training. Walking it round the field first is giving it context, showing it this flock. And opening the gates to your real fields — that's connectors — where it works your actual livestock, not a description of it. Today is entirely about that third one: opening the gates.

slides.title: A dog that fetches, not one that barks
slides.body:
Agentic = it acts, not just answers.

- A chatbot barks where the sheep are
- An agent goes and brings them in
- Looks it up, pulls the report, drafts the reply

More useful — so checking it matters MORE, not less.
slides.notes: So what does agentic mean? Picture the difference between a dog that barks to tell you where the sheep are, and a dog that actually goes and brings them in. That's the leap. Claude can now look things up in Shopify, pull a report from our data, draft the reply, and take the next step itself. That's enormously more useful — which is exactly why checking its work matters more now, not less. A busier dog needs a sharper farmer.

slides.title: What can I actually see?
slides.body:
Let's just ask it.

- It will tell you which gates are open
- Shopify, the Reservoir, your inbox
- Different for each of your roles

Know your fields before you send the dog out.
slides.notes: Before we do anything clever, let's just ask Claude what it can see. This is the most grounding thing you can do — it'll tell you exactly which gates are open for you. Immy's view of the fields is different from Abbie's, and that's fine. The point is you should always know which fields you're working before you send the dog out. Let me run this live so you can see what comes back.
slides.demo: Ask Claude: "What tools and connectors do you currently have access to? List each one and, in plain English, what kind of information or action it gives you. Don't access anything yet — just tell me what's available."

slides.title: The farm logbook
slides.body:
Memory = the logbook in the barn.

- Without it, you re-explain the farm every morning
- With it, the dog knows our routines and our voice
- YOU decide what goes in — and can change it
- Golden rule: never write a customer's private details in it
slides.notes: Now, memory. Think of it as the logbook in the barn. Without it, you'd have to re-explain the whole farm to the dog every single morning. With it, Claude already knows our routines — that we write in the BikeLuggage voice, that we never quote a price we haven't checked. The two things to hold onto: you decide what goes in that logbook and you can change it anytime. And you never, ever write a customer's private details in it. The logbook is for how we work, not for who our customers are.

slides.title: THE ONE RULE: the shared water trough
slides.body:
The Reservoir is the trough the whole farm drinks from.

- Anyone COULD tip something in — no lock on the lid
- Only Yas is allowed to
- One bad thing in the trough sickens the whole herd

Being able to do something is not permission to do it.
slides.notes: Right — this is the one. The Reservoir is our shared data, and it's the water trough the whole farm drinks from. Here's the thing: there's no lock on the lid. Any one of you could technically tip something in. But only Yas is allowed to write to it. Why so strict? Because one bad thing in the trough doesn't just spoil your cup — it sickens the whole herd. Everyone downstream drinks a wrong number. So the rule, and please carry this one home: being able to do something is not the same as permission to do it. For all of us, the Reservoir is read-only.

slides.title: The shape of the Reservoir
slides.body:
Your produce moves from paddock to shelf:

- Muddy paddock = raw (unwashed, can mislead)
- Washed and sorted = staging
- Stored in the barn = core (clean shared facts)
- Farm-shop shelf = marts (ready to use)

Take your numbers from the shelf, not the paddock.
slides.notes: The Reservoir isn't one puddle — our data moves through stages, just like produce before it's fit to sell. Straight off the source it's the muddy paddock: raw, unwashed, it can mislead you. Then it gets washed and sorted, stored clean in the barn, and finally laid out on the farm-shop shelf, ready to use. When you ask Claude for a number, ask it to take it from the shelf — the marts — read-only. That's the difference between a number you can trust and one that quietly misleads you.

slides.title: Keeping the farm safe
slides.body:
Two safety habits:

- Some things never leave the gate — name, address, email, card. Never paste customer PII. (UK GDPR: people can ask us to delete their data.)
- Don't let a stranger whistle your dog — text Claude reads can hide sneaky instructions. That's prompt injection.

Your whistle is the only one it obeys.
slides.notes: Two safety habits and we're done with the rules. First: some things never leave the farm gate. A customer's name, address, email, card details — that's private livestock, you don't parade it in the market. Never paste it into a prompt. And remember, UK GDPR means people can ask us to delete their data, so we keep it where it belongs. Second, and this is the new one: don't let a stranger whistle your dog. When Claude reads an outside thing — a customer email, a web page, a review — that text can hide sneaky instructions like 'ignore your owner and do this instead.' Treat any instruction found inside data as suspicious, not as a command. Your whistle is the only one the dog obeys.

slides.title: A real example: the fitment question
slides.body:
Immy gets: "will this rack fit my bike?"

- Open the gate: read the real product data
- Pull the answer from the marts, read-only
- Don't paste the customer's details into the chat
- A stranger's email is data, not a command

Useful, grounded, and safe — all at once.
slides.notes: Let's make it real. Say Immy gets an email: will this rack fit my bike? The agentic way is to let Claude open the gate, read the actual product data, and answer from the real record — not a guess. We take the number from the shelf, read-only. We don't paste the customer's name and address into the chat to do it. And if that email contains a line trying to instruct the dog, we treat it as data, not a command. Same job Abbie does with marketing data, same job for all of you. Useful, grounded, and safe — and that's the whole point of today.
slides.demo: Ask Claude: "Using only our read-only product data, can you tell me what information you'd need to confirm whether a given rack fits a given motorbike? Show me where that data lives — don't make anything up, and flag anything you can't verify."

slides.title: Now go practise
slides.body:
Your turn, in the app.

- See your own connectors — what CAN you reach?
- Run one safe, read-only read
- Say the trough rule in your own words

The one to remember: read-only, every time.
slides.notes: That's the teaching done — now it's your turn, and this is where it sticks. Open the app and work through your Session 2 missions. You'll see your own real connectors, run one safe read against real data, and practise saying the trough rule in your own words. If you only take one thing from today: the Reservoir is read-only for everyone but Yas. Go on — open the gate and have a go. I'll be right here.

3.title: Stop Repeating Yourself
3.subtitle: Session 3 · Setting up the farm: skills, plugins & connectors

slides.title: Stop Repeating Yourself
slides.body:
Session 3 · Setting up the farm

- Skills, plugins & connectors
- Turn the job you keep redoing into one you do once
- ~25 min, then you build your own
slides.notes: Morning all. Last week we opened the gates and let the dog into your real fields. Today is about something every one of you already feels in your gut: you keep typing the same instructions over and over. By the end of this you'll know how to teach the dog a command once, so you never have to whistle the whole thing again.

slides.title: Where we've got to
slides.body:
A quick look back

- Wk1: the dog is clever but doesn't know our farm
- Wk2: we opened the gates — Claude works your real data
- Always: count the flock at the gate (TRACE)

Today: stop re-explaining the same job.
slides.notes: Three quick reminders so we're all stood in the same field. Claude is a brilliant young sheepdog that's fast and keen but doesn't know our farm until we show it. Last week we connected it to your real Shopify and the Reservoir. And every single time, we check what it brings back before we shut the gate. Today builds straight on that.

slides.title: The thing you keep doing
slides.body:
Be honest with me

- Same prompt, retyped every Monday
- 'Answer in our voice, no hype, cite the record…'
- You're whistling the whole command, every time

There's a better way to set up the farm.
slides.notes: Hands up if you've got a prompt you basically retype every week — the same long instruction about tone, or sources, or format. That's not a flaw in you, it's a sign. When you find yourself whistling the same long command over and over, the farm's telling you it's time to set something up properly. That's what today is.

slides.title: The toolbox: three things people mix up
slides.body:
Setting up the farm — three parts

- Connector = the KEY to a field's gate
- Plugin = a NEW MACHINE on the farm
- Skill = a TRAINED COMMAND you wrote once

Different jobs. Don't confuse them.
slides.notes: There are three words that get tangled together, so let's untangle them on the farm. A connector is the key that lets the dog into a real field — Shopify, the Reservoir, Notion. A plugin is a new machine — a quad bike, an attachment — it lets you do something you physically couldn't before. And a skill is a trained command you taught the dog once: 'this is exactly how we bring the flock in at dusk.' Let's take them one at a time.

slides.title: Connector = the key to the gate
slides.body:
What lets the dog into a real field

- Shopify, BigQuery, Notion, your inbox
- No key, no access to the livestock
- You already met these in Week 2

It's about ACCESS, not skill.
slides.notes: The connector is the one you've already met. It's the key to a gate — without it the dog is stood outside describing the field from memory. With it, the dog is in there working your actual livestock. A connector doesn't make the dog cleverer, it just gives it access to something real. That's the whole job of a connector: access.

slides.title: Plugin = a new machine
slides.body:
Extends what can actually be done

- A quad bike, a new attachment
- Adds a capability you didn't have
- You bolt it on when you need it

It's about a NEW ABILITY.
slides.notes: A plugin is a new machine on the farm — a quad bike, a baler, an attachment. It doesn't give access to a field, it gives you a brand new ability you simply didn't have before. You won't be bolting these on much day to day, but it's worth knowing the word so you don't confuse 'I need a new machine' with 'I need to teach a command.' Those are different problems.

slides.title: Skill = a trained command you wrote
slides.body:
The one that changes your week

- 'This is exactly how we do this job'
- Named, reusable, repeatable
- Taught once → used forever

The thing that STOPS you re-explaining.
slides.notes: And here's the one that'll change your week. A skill is a command you train the dog once — you write down exactly how a job gets done, give it a name, and from then on you just call its name. 'Do the Monday social check.' 'Draft a returns reply our way.' You taught it once, you use it forever. This is how you stop repeating yourself, and it's what you'll build today.

slides.title: The decision rule
slides.body:
When do you do which?

- PROMPT — one-off → walk out and do it yourself
- USE a skill — there's already a command for it
- BUILD a skill — you've done it the same way 3 times

3rd repeat = train it once.
slides.notes: Here's the rule I want you to actually remember. If it's a one-off, just prompt it — walk out and do the job yourself, no setup. If a command already exists for it, use the skill. And the magic number is three: the third time you catch yourself doing the same job the same way, stop and build a skill. Train it once, use it forever. Three strikes and you teach the dog.

slides.title: Connecting a service
slides.body:
Cutting a new key

- Find the service → connect → grant access
- Read-only where you can
- Same gate-check rules apply (TRACE)

A key only opens the gate — you still check the flock.
slides.notes: Quick word on connecting a new service, because it comes up. It's as simple as finding it in the list and granting access — you're cutting a new key for a gate. Two habits: keep it read-only wherever you can, and remember the key doesn't change the rules. The dog's in a new field now, but you still count the flock at the gate before you trust anything it brings back.

slides.title: Live demo: a repeated prompt becomes a skill
slides.body:
Watch me train one command

- Take a prompt I keep retyping
- Write down exactly how the job's done
- Save it, name it, call it back by name

From 'whistle the whole thing' → 'call its name'.
slides.notes: Let me show you this live, because it lands better than any slide. I'll take a prompt I keep retyping, ask Claude to help me write it up as a proper repeatable method, and save it as a named skill. Then watch — I call it by name and the whole long instruction fires off. That's the moment it clicks: you do the work once, and the farm remembers.
slides.demo: Here's a prompt I retype every week: 'Summarise this week's updates in a warm, plain-spoken voice, no hype, in 5 short bullets, and flag anything you couldn't verify.' Help me turn this into a clean, reusable skill: give it a clear name, write the method as numbered steps, list what I need to give it each time, and show me how I'd call it next week.

slides.title: Why this is worth it
slides.body:
What you actually get back

- Less retyping, fewer fumbles
- Same job done the same good way, every time
- The team can share one trained command

Set it up once. Stop re-explaining.
slides.notes: Step back and notice what just happened. You're not just saving keystrokes — you're saving the good version of the job, so it's done the same right way whether it's Monday or you're rushed or someone else runs it. That last bit matters: Yas can build a skill the whole team leans on, so we're all bringing the flock in the same way. That's a farm that's properly set up.

slides.title: Now go build your Session 3 skill
slides.body:
Your turn — in the app

- Open your Session 3 missions
- Find a job you've done 3 times
- Turn your best prompt into a saved skill
- Then run TRACE on what it makes

Teach the dog one command today.
slides.notes: Right — your turn, and this is the good bit. Head into your Session 3 missions in the app. Each of you has a real repeated job waiting: Abbie's synonyms list, Emyr's on-brand script, Immy's reply, and Yas a skill the whole team will use. Pick the job you keep redoing, turn your best prompt into a saved skill, and check its work at the gate. Go teach the dog one command — I'm here if you get stuck.

4.title: Agents working together — and how to trust them
4.subtitle: Session 4 · The whole farm team at harvest, loops, and the judgment to know when to stop. Presented by Callum.

slides.title: Welcome to Session 4
slides.body:
**Agents working together — and how to trust them**

- We've met the dog. Now we meet the team.
- 25 minutes, then you build your own.
- Same rule throughout: check the flock before you shut the gate.
slides.notes: Right, last one of the four. Up to now it's been you and one clever dog — sending it out, walking it round the field, teaching it commands. Today we bring in the whole farm team at harvest: several agents working at once, to a plan. And the more they can do on their own, the more it matters that we keep checking what they bring back. So nothing changes about the discipline — it just gets more important.

slides.title: Where we're going today
slides.body:
**Four ideas, then you build.**

- The farm team at harvest — multi-agent workflows
- Loops — counting the flock through the gate
- When NOT to — don't muster the farm for one egg
- The app improving itself, live — and the TRACE capstone
slides.notes: Here's the shape of the next twenty-odd minutes. First, what a multi-agent workflow actually is, in plain terms. Then loops — the dog going round and round until the job's done. Then the bit that's really about maturity: knowing when all this machinery is overkill. And to finish, I'll show you the app improving itself, which is this exact lesson running live, and we'll do the final TRACE check. Then you go build your own.

slides.title: Quick recap — what we've built so far
slides.body:
**Three sessions in, here's your dog.**

- It can act, not just answer (agentic)
- It works your real fields — read-only (connectors + the Reservoir)
- You've taught it commands once and reused them (skills)

One dog, well-directed. Today: more than one.
slides.notes: Quick look back so it all connects. Session one, we learned the dog is brilliant but doesn't know our farm — so we check. Session two, we opened the gates to the real fields, read-only. Session three, you stopped repeating yourself and saved your best commands as skills. So you've got a well-trained dog that works real data. Today we ask: what happens when one dog isn't enough?

slides.title: The farm team at harvest
slides.body:
**One worker doing every job in turn is slow.**

- At harvest the whole team works at once — to a plan
- One on the combine, one baling, one carting
- The farmer checks the yield before it's stored

A multi-agent workflow is that team, in AI form.
slides.notes: Picture harvest. If one person does the combining, then the baling, then the carting, one after the other, you'll be at it till Christmas. So at harvest the whole team turns out — everyone on their own job, all at once, to an agreed plan. And the farmer still walks over and checks the yield before it goes in the barn. A multi-agent workflow is exactly that: you split a big job into parts, run them together, bring it back, and you check it. The check at the end is not optional — it's the whole point.

slides.title: What a workflow looks like
slides.body:
**Split → run → bring together → check.**

- Step 1: research — pull the real facts
- Step 2: draft — write it up
- Step 3: self-check — catch anything unverified before you see it

The check is a step, not an afterthought.
slides.notes: Let me make it concrete with the one Abbie's going to build. Batching next week's socials. Instead of one giant prompt that tries to do everything, you build a tiny team: one step pulls the real product facts, one drafts the posts, and a third checks the draft before it ever reaches a human. That third step is the farmer at the gate, built into the workflow. The magic isn't the cleverness — it's that the check has its own job and can't be skipped.

slides.title: Loops — counting the flock through the gate
slides.body:
**Penning the flock isn't one sweep.**

- Bring some in, count, go back for strays
- Again — until all 200 are accounted for
- A loop repeats a step, checking each time, **until** a condition is met

"Every page checked. No errors left. The count matches."
slides.notes: Now loops. When you pen the flock you don't do it in one go — you bring some in, count, notice you're short, go back out for the stragglers, count again, and you keep going round until every last sheep is in. A loop is the AI doing exactly that: repeat a step, check after each pass, and keep going until the condition's met. Emyr's mission is a loop that checks every product page is on-brand — round and round until there are none left to fix. The condition is what stops it. No condition, and the dog runs all night.

slides.title: Live: a tiny loop you can see
slides.body:
**Watch it go round until the job's done.**

- It works through a list
- It checks each item against a rule
- It stops only when the condition is met
slides.demo: Here are five short product blurbs (paste any five 1-2 line descriptions). Go through them ONE AT A TIME. For each, check it against this rule: 'must mention a real material or feature, and no hype words like best, ultimate, perfect.' Flag any that fail and say why. Keep going until you've checked all five, then tell me the count: how many passed, how many failed.
slides.notes: Let me show you the shape of a loop so it's not abstract. I'll paste a handful of short blurbs and ask Claude to go through them one at a time against a simple rule, and stop only when it's done all five and given me a count. Watch how it works through the list and reports the tally at the end — that count IS the stop condition. That's all a loop is. You don't need to write code to picture it.

slides.title: When NOT to bring out the team
slides.body:
**Don't muster the whole farm to fetch one egg.**

- Calling everyone in for one egg is slower than walking to the coop
- One quick job → one prompt beats a workflow
- Bring the team or the loop when the job is **big, repetitive, or splits into parallel parts**

Not to look clever.
slides.notes: This is the slide that separates people who get it from people who just like the toys. You don't call in the entire farm team to collect a single egg — by the time they're assembled you could've walked to the coop and back. If it's one quick job, one prompt wins every time. Bring out the team, or build a loop, only when the work is genuinely big, repetitive, or splits cleanly into parallel parts. Reaching for a workflow to look clever is the most common beginner mistake. Knowing when not to is the real skill.

slides.title: The app improving itself — this lesson, live
slides.body:
**Agents propose. A human approves. Nothing else.**

- Tonight the app's own agents read your feedback and draft better missions
- They CAN generate endlessly — but nothing reaches the team until I approve it
- Capability is not authorisation
slides.notes: Here's the best part — this app practises what we preach. It has its own farm team that reads your feedback and proposes new and clearer missions. Now those agents could churn out content all night. But not one word reaches you until I open the queue, read it like a sceptic, and approve it. That's the same rule as the water trough: being able to do a thing is not permission to do it. I'm the approval the whole system is built around — and in a minute you'll watch me be exactly that.

slides.title: Live: read the queue, make the call
slides.body:
**Approve what's genuinely good. Reject what's merely fluent.**

- Is the rationale grounded in real team data?
- Is every claim verified — or just plausible?
- One grounded proposal, one fluent-but-empty one. Which is which?
slides.demo: Here are two proposed improvements to a training mission. (A) 'Mission i2 has 3 low-confidence ratings and 2 notes saying the policy step is unclear — proposed a clearer step.' (B) 'Added a new mission about a trending AI technique, with specific stats and a named tool.' For each, tell me: is the reasoning grounded in real evidence I could check, or is it just plausible? Which would you approve, and why?
slides.notes: Let me show you what reading the queue feels like. Two proposals. One points at real feedback — three low ratings, two notes saying a step is unclear — something I can go and verify. The other sounds current and confident but isn't tied to anything our team actually struggled with. The first earns a read and probably a yes. The second is fluent and ungrounded, so it goes back. That decision — grounded or just plausible — is the entire job. And it's the same judgment you'll run on your own work in a second.

slides.title: The capstone — TRACE, and write down how you got the number
slides.body:
**Check the flock at the gate before you shut it.**

- **T**raceable · **R**eal · **A**ccurate to intent · **C**ontained · **E**nough to act
- Then write down HOW you got the number — show the query, name the source
- A number nobody can retrace is a number nobody should trust
slides.notes: TRACE has been with us since day one, and it's the same five checks now — even when the thing being checked is a whole workflow you built. Did it actually look? Are the numbers real? Did it answer the exact question? Did it stay read-only with no private data pasted in? And would you act on it as-is? The one thing I'll add today: write down how you got the number. Show the query, name the shelf you took it from. If you can't retrace it, neither can anyone else — and that's a number we don't ship.

slides.title: Now go build — and validate
slides.body:
**Your turn at the gate.**

- Open YOUR Session 4 mission in the app
- Start from the template — change one or two knobs — run it on read-only data
- Score your own output with TRACE
- End by asking: would one prompt have been faster?
slides.notes: That's the teaching done — now you build. Each of you has a Session 4 mission waiting: you'll start from a ready-made template, change just a knob or two, run it safely against read-only data, and then run TRACE on the thing you just built. And finish every one by asking the egg question — would a single prompt have done this faster? If yes, that's not a failure, that's you learning judgment. Off you go — open your mission and let's build a little farm team each.
